retrieved_docs.title: Doppler Liver Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation of Results -- Indications -- Diagnosis of Portal Hypertension and Patency of Collaterals
retrieved_docs.content: Additional strengths of Doppler assessment include real-time observation, while limitations include a poor detection of slow blood flow and a reduced frame rate. [18] Other useful parameters in the diagnosis of cirrhosis include liver and spleen size, evaluation of liver echotechture, and the evaluation of the bluntness of the liver edge. [19] The normal portal vein pressure is less than 6 mmHg. Portal hypertension is clinically manifested when this pressure exceeds 12 mmHg.

retrieved_docs.title: Doppler Liver Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation of Results -- Indications -- Diagnosis of Portal Hypertension and Patency of Collaterals
retrieved_docs.content: Cirrhosis and an alteration in portal hemodynamics ultimately result in an increased pressure gradient in the portal vein resulting in abnormal portal vein blood flow away from the liver. [16] Doppler ultrasonography can assess blood flow direction and evaluate the presence of collateral vessels. Doppler indices include portal and splenic venous velocities, flow directions, and resistive/pulsatility indices of the arterial vasculature. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Liver Disease -- Evaluation -- Radiologic Investigations
retrieved_docs.content: An upper endoscopy can diagnose and treat esophageal varices. On endoscopy, we can measure the size of varices. Small varices are less than 5 mm, and large varices are greater than 5 mm.

retrieved_docs.title: Portal Hypertension -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasound and computed tomography (CT) scan of the abdomen can identify signs of portal hypertension, which include splenomegaly and portosystemic collaterals, cirrhotic changes in the liver, and ascites. [12] Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), enhanced with Gadolinium, can detect esophageal varices. [13] Doppler ultrasound of the portal vein can detect the presence of stenosis or thrombosis. The Doppler ultrasound study of the portal vein will show either hepatopetal portal vein flow (toward the liver) or hepatofugal portal vein flow (away from the liver), depending on the degree of portal hypertension. Hepatopetal flow is normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Doppler Liver Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation of Results -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic Doppler ultrasound is a non-invasive investigative tool that can help clinicians, especially if added to an abdominal ultrasound exam. The procedure is cost-effective and has no complications. Doppler ultrasound is the first imaging technique used in assessing the vascular condition in the following diseases: Portal hypertension associated with cirrhosis Portal or hepatic vein thrombosis [38] Vascularity of the hepatic focal lesions [39] Follow-up after TIPS or liver transplantation [40] Measuring the hepatic vein and artery patency, blood velocity, and direction

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic variceal ligation should be repeated every 1 to 4 weeks until varices are eradicated. If TIPS is done, repeat endoscopy to assess rebleeding. Endoscopic screening should be done in patients with known cirrhosis every two to three years and yearly in patients with decompensated cirrhosis. Patients with liver stiffness less than 20 kPa and with platelets greater than 150,000 can avoid endoscopic screening and may follow up by annual TE and platelet count. Prognosis In cirrhosis, one-year survival is 50% for those surviving two weeks following a variceal bleed. In-hospital mortality remains high related to the severity of underlying cirrhosis, ranging from 0% in Child A to 32% in Child C disease. Prognosis in noncirrhotic portal fibrosis is better than for cirrhotics. Complications

retrieved_docs.title: Doppler Liver Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation of Results -- Indications -- Evaluation of a TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) Stent
retrieved_docs.content: The transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) procedure is one treatment option for patients with cirrhosis or noncirrhotic total hypertension. TIPS shunts function to treat those who suffer from refractory ascites and variceal bleeding. [25] TIPS dysfunction has a high incidence and can be secondary to malpositioning, thrombosis, and pseudointimal hyperplasia. [26] Ultrasound can assess TIPS velocities(normally considered 90 to 190 cm/s), normal portal vein blood velocity before entering the shunt (normal reading is around 30 cm/s), and assess waveforms and the presence of stenosis. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Cirrhosis -- Evaluation -- Imaging and Liver Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: Several imaging modalities are used alongside labs to help diagnose cirrhosis. These include ultrasound, CT, MRI, and transient elastography (fibroscan). Ultrasonography is a cheap, noninvasive, and available modality for evaluating cirrhosis. It can detect nodularity and increased echogenicity of the liver, which are seen in cirrhosis; however, it is nonspecific as these findings can also be seen in fatty liver. [21] It can also determine the ratio of the caudate lobe width to the right lobe width, which usually increases in cirrhosis. [22] Moreover, it is a useful screening tool for HCC in cirrhotic patients. Duplex Doppler ultrasonography helps to assess the patency of hepatic, portal, and mesenteric veins. CT and MRI, in contrast, can detect HCC and vascular lesions, with MRI being superior to CT. [23] MRI can also be used to detect the level of iron and fat deposition in the liver for hemochromatosis, steatosis, and biliary obstruction if an MRC (magnetic resonance cholangiography) is obtained.[24] [25] MRI, however, is expensive and not readily available. Transient elastography (fibroscan) is a noninvasive method that uses high-velocity ultrasound waves to measure liver stiffness, which correlates with fibrosis. In cirrhosis, a colloid liver spleen scan using technetium-99m sulfur colloid may show increased colloid uptake in the bone marrow and spleen compared to the liver. The presence of varices in the esophagus or stomach on esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) suggests portal hypertension. A liver biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosing cirrhosis and assessing the disease's degree of inflammation (grade) and fibrosis (stage). See Image . Cirrhosis, Portal Space in Fibrous Septa. Reticulin stain 4×.

retrieved_docs.title: Doppler Liver Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation of Results -- Technique or Treatment -- Limitations
retrieved_docs.content: Obesity can cause limitations in the visualization of vessel flow and velocity. Eating may cause an increase in the portal pressure and widen the diameter of the vessel. Ingested contents and resultant gaseous distension may hinder the visualization of vessels. Therefore, fasting for at least 4 to 6 hours before the Doppler examination is recommended. Changes in the liver hemodynamics may be falsely diagnosed if the gain and frequency are not well adjusted in the device. The low frequency may falsely diagnose portal vein thrombosis. Patients who cannot hold their breath will create some difficulty for the operator to visualize the vessels, but this could be overcome by the timing of inhalation and moving the probe synchronously.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

Integrated assessment of alcohol use, liver disease, variceal formation, and appropriate endoscopic or radiologic interventions is essential for optimal patient care in esophageal and related vascular disorders.

Gastrointestinal hemorrhage at the esophagus is classified as upper gastrointestinal bleeding (نَزِيف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي) and may arise from variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Diagnostic evaluation includes an upper gastrointestinal X‑ray performed directly at the esophagus. Therapeutic endoscopic interventions include esophageal variceal ligation (ربط دوالي المريء) and esophageal sclerotherapy (مفاغرة مريئية), which belong to other non‑operating‑room upper GI therapeutic procedures. Esophageal disorders are linked clinically to alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول) and alcoholic liver disease (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ); the latter encompasses liver cirrhosis (تشَمُّع كَبِدي) and is associated with esophageal variceal bleeding. Understanding the interconnected network of dilated‑vein morphology (وريد متدلي), anatomical sites (مفاغرة مريئية), procedural approaches, and etiological factors (alcohol use, liver disease) is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1143
retrieved_docs.content: Varices van overige lokalisaties is a classification that includes دوالي مريئية بدون ذكر نزيف (esophageal varices without reported bleeding) and نزيف دَوالِي المريء (bleeding esophageal varices). تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic cirrhosis) is clinically associated with نزيف دَوالِي المريء, indicating that patients with cirrhosis are at heightened risk for esophageal variceal bleeding. تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ (alcoholic cirrhosis) is a subtype of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي, linking alcohol‑related liver disease to the broader condition of hepatic cirrhosis and its associated risk of bleeding esophageal varices. This interconnected knowledge underscores the importance of monitoring variceal status in cirrhotic patients, particularly those with an alcoholic etiology, to guide appropriate clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2460
retrieved_docs.content: Varices van overige lokalisaties is a broader category that includes نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding). تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (liver cirrhosis) is clinically associated with نزيف دَوالِي المريء, indicating that patients with liver cirrhosis are at heightened risk for esophageal variceal bleeding. تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ (alcoholic cirrhosis) is a specific subtype of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي, making it a child concept under liver cirrhosis. Together, these relationships emphasize that variceal bleeding can arise as a complication of both general and alcohol‑related liver cirrhosis, underscoring the need for careful monitoring and management of patients with cirrhotic disease.

Alcohol‑related liver disease is a key etiologic factor for both esophageal and gastric varices, thereby increasing the risk of variceal hemorrhage and its sequelae. Correction of esophageal atresia (Correction of esophageal atresia) further illustrates the clinical relevance of operative management within the spectrum of esophageal disorders. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: Esophagoscopy for injection of esophageal varices is a therapeutic endoscopic procedure used to treat دوالي المَريء (esophageal varices), which are located at المفاغرة المريئية (the esophageal junction) and represent a manifestation of وريد متدلي (dangling vein) within the spectrum of esophageal disorders. دوالي المَريء serve as a parent condition for نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding) and are pathophysiologically linked to دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة (intestinal varices) and varices van overige lokalisaties (varices of other locations), all sharing a common vascular component classified under Other specified diseases of veins and lymphatics. These venous dilations arise secondary to portal hypertension caused by chronic تعاطي الكحول (alcohol consumption) and underlying liver disease captured by الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد (SMQ) (liver failure, cirrhosis, hepatic fibrosis, and related conditions). Consequently, دوالي المَريء and دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة are frequent sources of gastrointestinal hemorrhage, particularly upper‑GI bleeding, and require interventional strategies such as ربط دوالي المريء (band ligation) and other esophageal therapeutic procedures to achieve hemostasis. The clinical picture is further complicated by the association of chronic alcohol use with traumatic injuries such as closed head injury and ankle fracture, underscoring the need for integrated diagnostic and management approaches that address liver pathology, variceal disease, bleeding risk, and alcohol‑related morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 961
retrieved_docs.content: associated with نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding), underscoring the risk of bleeding complications in patients with advanced hepatic disease. Overall, the knowledge base connects variceal conditions of the esophagus, broader esophageal disorders, venous and lymphatic disease classifications, alcohol‑related liver injury, and severe liver disease, illustrating how these entities interrelate in the context of diagnosis, classification, and clinical risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2053
retrieved_docs.content: Varices van overige lokalisaties is classified as an esophageal disorder and includes as a child the condition نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding). Esophageal disorders are clinically associated with alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول) and with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ), and they have a direct relationship with نزيف دَوالِي المريء. Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a subtype of hepatic cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي); both alcoholic cirrhosis and hepatic cirrhosis are clinically associated with نزيف دَوالِي المريء. Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcoholic cirrhosis and also with alcohol consumption. The entity “Without” is noted as being associated with esophageal disorders, although its clinical relevance is unclear. This interconnected network emphasizes that esophageal variceal bleeding commonly occurs in the context of cirrhosis—particularly alcoholic cirrhosis—and that alcohol use and its hepatic complications may be reflected by abnormal chest imaging, highlighting the need to assess alcohol exposure and liver disease when managing esophageal disorders and related hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: of esophageal disorders. Within esophageal disorders, Varices van overige lokalisaties (a child of esophageal disorders) is linked to intestinal varices (دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة) and has a child entity of esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول) and alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) are clinically associated with hepatic cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي) and the broader spectrum of liver failure, fibrosis, cirrhosis and other liver‑damage conditions (الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد (SMQ)). These liver diseases predispose to esophageal varices and subsequent variceal bleeding. Therapeutic management of esophageal disorders includes esophageal dilatation (esophageal dilatation) performed directly on the esophagus and classified under both “الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالمريء” and “عملية في المريء”. Child procedures within this group are esophageal variceal ligation (ربط دوالي المريء) and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: (التَلَيُّف), hepatic infiltration (تَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد), and other liver‑damage states (SMQ), which are members of the esophageal varices category, underscoring the close relationship between hepatic disease and variceal formation. Therapeutic options for these conditions are organized under esophageal therapeutic procedures (الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالمريء) and include esophageal variceal ligation (ربط دوالي المريء) and esophageal sclerotherapy (مفاغرة مريئية). Collectively, these entities and their interrelationships define a comprehensive framework in which esophageal and lower‑gastrointestinal variceal conditions, their vascular morphology, associated risk factors, and endoscopic therapies are integrated for improved diagnostic understanding and patient management.
